exemplar.title:
To what extent does GDP per capita impact total fertility 
rates within different countries?

detailedExemplarData.title:
To what extent does GDP per capita impact total fertility 
rates within different countries?

subject.title: Environmental systems and societies (ESS - Old)
subject.description: IB Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) examines environmental challenges through an interdisciplinary approach combining natural sciences with social and economic perspectives. Students study ecosystems, biodiversity, resource management, pollution, climate change, and sustainability using systems thinking and collaborative investigation. The course develops environmental literacy and critical thinking skills essential for understanding and addressing complex environmental issues facing our planet.

strengths: Well structured with clear headings and logical sequencing throughout
Consistent use of appropriate ESS terminology and clear presentation of the regression equation
weaknesses: Occasional verbosity and stylistic inconsistencies reduce concision
Notational errors (e.g., written as Râ»Â²) and minor formatting issues impede full coherence
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The reportâs organization and use of ESS terminology are strengths, but lapses in concision and notation hinder clarity in parts.  This aligns with the mid-band descriptors and corresponds to 2 marks out of 3 when scaled to the overall total.

strengths: Clear, focused research question precisely identifying GDP per capita and fertility rate
Accurate use of the Demographic Transition Model to contextualize population dynamics
weaknesses: Environmental context remains largely socio-economic with only implied ecological feedbacks
Connections to ecosystem services and carrying-capacity are not made explicit
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the student formulates an excellent, focused research question and uses theory (DTM) effectively, the environmental context and explicit links to ecosystem services are only partially developed.  This performance aligns with the midâband descriptors, warranting 2 marks out of 6 when scaled to the 10âpoint total.

strengths: Well-justified stratified sampling strategy by income level reducing selection bias
Precise definitions of independent and dependent variables support reproducibility
weaknesses: No risk assessment or ethical considerations are provided
Method lacks detail on software tools, data-cleaning protocols and exclusion criteria beyond year selection
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The plan includes a repeatable overall method and a strong justification for stratified sampling, but omits critical details (risks/ethics and procedural specifics) required for full IB compliance.  This places the work in the lower half of the rubric, resulting in 1 mark out of 6 scaled appropriately.

strengths: Evaluation includes a concise table summarizing method strengths, limitations and improvements
Discussion acknowledges moderate correlation and cultural exceptions, deepening analysis
Meaningful suggestions (e.g., multivariate and longitudinal studies) address major weaknesses
weaknesses: Policy and sustainability implications are only partially developed
Feasibility considerations (data availability, timeline) for proposed improvements are not discussed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student offers a sound evaluation with clear identification of strengths, weaknesses and realistic improvements, but stops short of fully contextualizing in policy and feasibility terms.  This quality aligns with the mid-band and scales to 2 marks out of 6.

strengths: Scatter plot is well labelled with regression curve, equation and RÂ²
Correct interpretation of the negative coefficient, linking back to hypotheses
Clear justification for choosing graphical presentation over raw tables
weaknesses: Axes lack units and region-coding, and no error bars or raw data tables are provided
Statistical uncertainty measures (p-values, confidence intervals, residuals) are omitted
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Results are presented clearly and analyzed correctly, and the conclusion is data-supported.  However, key quantitative details and full data presentation are missing.  This performance sits in the mid-range of the rubric and scales to 2 marks out of 6.

strengths: Application (education and family planning) is directly justified by the findings
Mechanistic link between evidence and solution is clearly articulated
weaknesses: Implementation challenges, economic costs and environmental trade-offs are not evaluated
Discussion lacks depth on scalability and potential unintended consequences
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The proposed application is well-linked to the data, but the evaluation of strengths, weaknesses and broader trade-offs is incomplete.  This performance corresponds to the lower end of the 0â3 band, scaled to 1 mark.

strandSelections.reasoning: Appropriate ESS terminology (e.g., demographic transition, stratified sampling) is used accurately, but writing is occasionally verbose and contains stylistic inconsistencies.  This satisfies either correctness or concision but not both consistently, fitting band 2.

The evaluation section recognises the moderate correlation, absence of causation, and influence of cultural factors, partially relating these to sustainability implications.  Some aspects (e.g., specific policy significance) remain underdeveloped, giving a satisfactory but not comprehensive evaluationâmid 3-4 band.

A data-supported conclusion is drawn, referencing the negative correlation, coefficient magnitude, and examples (Israel vs UK).  While generally valid, the argument would be stronger with quantitative uncertainty discussion and explicit reference back to environmental context.  Hence a mark at the top of the 3-4 band is justified.

The student calculates and interprets a logarithmic regression, presents the coefficient and RÂ², and links them to the hypothesis.  Nonetheless, no statistical significance test, confidence intervals, or residual analysis is included, leaving the analysis incomplete.  Thus the strand sits solidly in the âanalyzes correctly but incompletelyâ range.

The student proposes including additional variables, expanding years, and employing multivariate regressionâmeaningful steps that address major stated weaknesses.  However, depth on feasibility or methodological detail is limited, keeping the work within, not at the top of, the 3-4 bracket.

The method states source (World Bank), variables, year, choice of logarithmic regression and scatter plot.  These steps are repeatable, but finer procedural details (software, data-cleaning rules, exclusion criteria beyond âmost recent yearâ) are missing, and no justification is given for choosing 2019.  Consequently the plan may not collect *sufficient* relevant data for all research aims, aligning with the mid-band (3-4).

Connections between the environmental issue (sustainable population size, resource pressure) and the research question are described through theory (DTM) and examples (education, healthcare, gender equality).  While the link is explained, it omits a few aspects such as explicit feedbacks to ecosystem services.  The work therefore fits âdescribes connectionsâ¦ with some omissions,â meriting the upper mark of the 3-4 band.

The student formulates a clear, single-sentence research question that precisely identifies the two variables (GDP per capita and total fertility rate) and the scope (across different countries). The wording is sufficiently narrow to allow focused data collection and statistical treatment, and it avoids unnecessary breadth (for example, no temporal dimension is introduced). Hence the question is both relevant to ESS and coherent/focused, matching the 5-6 band.

The discussion highlights strengths (population stability, gender equality) but offers only a brief mention of implementation challenges; environmental trade-offs or economic costs are not explored.  Partial evaluation aligns with 2 marks (band 2).

The report follows the prescribed IA structure with clear headings, logical sequencing, and signposting, maintaining organization from context through evaluation.  Minor formatting issues (occasional long paragraphs, image placement) do not disrupt overall structure, fulfilling the top band.

Strengths (World Bank reliability, large sample) and weaknesses (single year, missing variables) are identified and described in prose and table form.  Limitations such as potential logarithmic model misfit or data heteroscedasticity are omitted, so the discussion is sound but not exhaustive, warranting 3 marks.

The introduction explains why population dynamics influence sustainability and resource use, cites specific examples (South Korea, developed vs. developing nations) and links these to the importance of the study.  However, the environmental dimension is treated in a largely socio-economic frame; issues such as ecological carrying capacity or footprint are only implied.  The discussion is adequate but not developed in depth, so the strand is best described as âoutlines an environmental issue that provides context,â top of the 3-4 band.

The student explicitly states the use of stratified sampling by World-Bank income groups, explains that this captures variation at different development stages, and justifies exclusion of countries lacking both variables to maintain consistency.  This constitutes a reasoned justification rather than a mere description, fulfilling the 5-6 descriptor (awarded 5).

No risk assessment or ethical considerations are included.  Although secondary data generally present minimal risk, IB expects these aspects to be *addressed*; the omission places the work in the 0-mark band.

A labelled scatter plot with regression curve displays all 152 data points clearly and includes the equation and RÂ².  However, raw data or tables are absent, axes lack units in the graphic copy, and no error bars/categories are provided.  These omissions prevent full compliance with the 5-6 band, so the upper mark of 3-4 is appropriate.

A single solutionâexpanding education and family planningâis clearly linked to findings (education lowers fertility) and is justified through discussion of mechanisms and evidence.  This meets the descriptor of âjustifies one potential application/solution,â earning full marks for the strand.

The argument generally progresses logically; however, some sections (applications, large background segments) are repetitive, and equation notation shows minor errors (R^-2).  These lapses make parts difficult to follow, so âmainly logical but difficult to follow in partsââband 2âapplies.

annotations.comment: The introduction effectively links population dynamics to socioeconomic stability, but it would benefit from explicit discussion of ecological carrying capacity or environmental footprint to deepen the environmental context.
The hypothesis is stated clearly and links variables, but acknowledges unmeasured factors; consider reframing to focus solely on measurable relationships or detailing how proxies will be used.
Data collection specifies year 2019 but does not justify this choice; explain why 2019 is most representative or if data gaps influenced this decision.
The rationale for using a logarithmic regression model is succinctly explained and aligns well with the expected non-linear relationship.
The scatter plot lacks axis labels with units and region-coding, which impedes full interpretation; add units (USD, births per woman) and color-coding for income groups.
The regression results include equation $$y=8.72-0.69\ln(x)$$ and $$R^{-2}$$ terminology; correct notation to $$R^2$$ and discuss confidence intervals for full rigor.
The research question is clearly stated and focused, precisely identifying GDP per capita and fertility rates across countries, which facilitates a targeted and coherent investigation.
Your second paragraph outlines socio-economic impacts but only implies environmental consequences; explicitly linking to resource depletion or ecosystem services would strengthen context.
The Demographic Transition Model (DTM) is described accurately with clear stage definitions, which provides strong theoretical context for your research.
Discussion of education, healthcare, and gender equality is thorough, but ensure each claim is consistently cited to strengthen academic rigor and traceability.
Definitions of independent and dependent variables are precise and well-structured, supporting a reproducible design.
Listing potential influencers is insightful, but as these are not measured, discuss how their omission may bias results and propose partial proxy measures.
The stratified sampling by income level is well justified, clearly capturing variation across development stages and reducing selection bias.
The regression equation $$y = a + b\cdot\ln(x)$$ is clearly presented with variable definitions, enhancing communication of analytical methods.

The method overview mentions using World Bank data and scatter plots but omits software tools, data-cleaning protocols, and exclusion criteria beyond year choiceâadding these details makes the method fully repeatable.
The background section clearly defines GDP per capita and fertility rate using authoritative sources, demonstrating precise and accurate communication of key concepts.
While social and economic factors are well presented, the link to environmental sustainability remains implicit; consider adding explicit examples of environmental pressures.
Choosing a scatter plot over a data table is justified effectively, improving visual clarity of trends across 152 countries.
The conclusion effectively summarizes findings and aligns with hypotheses, reinforcing the negative correlation between GDP and fertility.
The report restates theoretical links to education, healthcare, and gender equality in conclusion; consider tightening to emphasize environmental sustainability.
The evaluation section identifies key limitations and suggests multivariate regression, demonstrating solid method evaluation, but feasibility details are limited.
Interpretation correctly links the negative coefficient to hypothesis support, showing how $$b=-0.69$$ reflects decreasing fertility with rising GDP.
Your interpretation acknowledges the moderate correlation and cultural exceptions (Israel vs UK), which enhances depth of discussion.
While qualitative examples enrich interpretation, include statistical uncertainty measures (p-values, confidence intervals) to fully support conclusions.
The application of education and family planning is directly justified by findings, demonstrating a clear link between evidence and proposed solution.
The table succinctly summarizes strengths, limitations, and improvements, aiding clarity of evaluation.
The improvements column proposes longitudinal and multivariate analyses, which address major weaknesses, but discuss practical considerations like data availability and timeline.
Solution evaluation omits discussion of implementation challenges, costs, and environmental trade-offs; include these aspects to fully evaluate your proposal.
The references list is varied and authoritative, but ensure consistent formatting of DOIs, URLs, and access dates for full academic convention.
